question: How big is India's economy?

theme.label: Economy and public money
readerQuestions: What is the headline economic number?
Is this a total number or a per-person number?
How much of the story is prices, exchange rates, or GDP share?
What does this explain about household life?

visualPlan.title: GDP, nominal
visualPlan.recommendedVisualization: single-series line chart with latest value and first-to-latest change
visualPlan.mobileGuidance: Use a line chart with large labels, sparse ticks, and a short reader caption.
visualPlan.whyThisMatters: This is the chart that answers the page question most directly.

plannedCharts.title: India's economy, 1950-51 to today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline answer: the total rupee size of everything India produces in a year.

plannedCharts.title: How big, compared with the world
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · India's GDP at market exchange rates vs at purchasing-power parity
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Whether India is the 5th or the 3rd largest economy depends entirely on which ruler you use.

plannedCharts.title: Where India sits among the big economies
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · GDP at market exchange rates, 1960 to 2024
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Size made concrete: the economies India has just caught or passed.

plannedCharts.title: Nominal vs real: how much is just prices?
plannedCharts.subtitle: MOSPI · GDP at current prices vs at constant 2011-12 prices
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single most misread thing about GDP: most of the headline number's growth is inflation.

plannedCharts.title: GDP per person
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Divide the economy by 1.4 billion people and the picture changes completely.

plannedCharts.title: Big economy, small incomes
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · GDP per capita at market exchange rates, India vs Asian peers
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The other half of the story: enormous in total, near the bottom per person.

plannedCharts.title: What India makes: from farms to services
plannedCharts.subtitle: MOSPI · share of gross value added by sector
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The deepest change in the Indian economy since independence: what it produces.

plannedCharts.title: The factories that never came
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's most important economic gap: it grew rich in services while skipping the manufacturing stage East Asia built on.

plannedCharts.title: Who spends: consumption, investment, trade
plannedCharts.subtitle: MOSPI · expenditure components as a share of GDP
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The same GDP, seen as who spends the money rather than who produces it.

plannedCharts.title: Produced here, but whose income?
plannedCharts.subtitle: MOSPI · GDP (output inside India) vs GNI (income to Indians)
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: GDP counts what is produced inside India; some of that income belongs to foreigners, and some Indian income comes from abroad.

plannedCharts.title: Who saves the money India invests
plannedCharts.subtitle: MOSPI · gross saving by who does the saving
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India saves about 30% of GDP — among the highest rates anywhere — and that saving is what funds investment.

plannedCharts.title: How fast it grows, year to year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The size is one thing; the speed is what makes headlines.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
